content.text: Why Open-Source Governance Matters for Claude Applications

A Claude application is not just the code your team writes and the API calls you make. It is a stack of open-source dependencies: HTTP clients, vector databases, embedding libraries, RAG frameworks, prompt templating libraries, data serialisation tools, and more. Each dependency carries a license that imposes obligations — on distribution, attribution, modification disclosure, and in the most demanding cases, on the licensing of your own proprietary code.

The AI ecosystem has a particular open-source concentration risk. Many of the most popular LangChain, LlamaIndex, and Haystack components are MIT or Apache 2.0 licensed, creating a permissive baseline. But some AI-adjacent infrastructure (databases, model serving frameworks, annotation tools) uses AGPL or SSPL, which have dramatically stronger obligations that can propagate to your entire application if not managed carefully.

AGPL 3.0 closes the 'SaaS loophole' of GPL: if you run AGPL-licensed software as a network service, users who interact with it are entitled to the source code of the combined work — including your proprietary Claude integration layer. A single AGPL dependency in a SaaS Claude application can trigger an obligation to release all application source.

attrs.alt: Three-tier OSS license classification: Tier 1 Permissive (MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD) approved without review; Tier 2 Weak Copyleft (LGPL, MPL) approved with review; Tier 3 Strong Copyleft (GPL, AGPL, SSPL) requiring legal exception

content.text: Policy Structure for Claude Application Dependencies

An OSS usage policy for Claude applications defines which licenses are pre-approved, which require case-by-case legal review, and which are prohibited without an explicit exception. The policy must cover both direct dependencies and transitive dependencies — a library you directly depend on may itself pull in a GPL-licensed transitive dependency.
The following licenses are approved for use in Claude applications without requiring legal review, provided attribution obligations are met:

Retain the copyright notice and license text in all distributions. For SaaS applications that do not distribute binaries, the obligation is typically to include the notice in the application's open-source acknowledgements page.
Same attribution requirements as MIT, plus a patent grant clause that protects users from patent claims by the licensor. Also requires noting any modifications to Apache 2.0-licensed files.
Same as MIT. The 3-Clause adds a non-endorsement clause (you may not use the licensor's name to endorse your product without permission — generally not an issue in practice).
Functionally equivalent to BSD 2-Clause; common in Node.js ecosystem dependencies.

The following licenses require a one-time legal review when first introduced as a dependency. Once reviewed and approved for a specific use pattern, they do not require re-review for the same usage pattern in other projects:

Permissible for use as a library (linked, not modified). Modifications to the LGPL-licensed library itself must be released. If you statically link an LGPL library, you must allow users to relink with a modified version — typically achieved by dynamic linking.

File-level copyleft: only modifications to MPL-licensed files must be released. Your files that interact with MPL code remain proprietary. Requires legal review to confirm the file-level boundary is preserved in your specific integration.
Similar to MPL 2.0 at the module level. Common in Java/Kotlin ecosystem components.
The following licenses are restricted. No Claude application dependency may use these licenses without an explicit written exception approved by Legal:

Strong copyleft: if your application is distributed, the combined work may be subject to GPL. For SaaS applications that do not distribute, the GPL SaaS loophole may apply — but this requires legal analysis before assuming it is safe.

Closes the SaaS loophole. If AGPL code runs as a network service and users interact with it, the combined work must be made available under AGPL. This is the highest-risk license category for SaaS Claude applications.

MongoDB's license, more restrictive than AGPL: the SSPL requires releasing not just your application but also all software used to offer the service (management, provisioning, monitoring tools). Used by MongoDB Community, Elasticsearch in some distributions.

Restricts commercial sale of the software. Usually combined with MIT or Apache — but the Commons Clause addition removes OSI-approved open-source status and adds commercial restrictions that may conflict with your business model.

import subprocess, json

def scan_python_dependencies(requirements_file: str = "requirements.txt") -> dict:
    """
    Scan Python project dependencies for license compliance using pip-licenses.
    Categorises each package into Tier 1/2/3 based on the OSS usage policy.
    """
    TIER1 = {"MIT", "Apache Software License", "Apache 2.0", "BSD License",
             "BSD 2-Clause", "BSD 3-Clause", "ISC License", "ISC", "Unlicense"}
    TIER2 = {"GNU Lesser General Public License v2 or later (LGPLv2+)",
             "GNU Lesser General Public License v3 (LGPLv3)",
             "Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)",
             "Eclipse Public License 2.0 (EPL-2.0)"}
    TIER3 = {"GNU General Public License v2 (GPLv2)",
             "GNU General Public License v3 (GPLv3)",
             "GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPLv3)",
             "Server Side Public License"}

    result = subprocess.run(
        ["pip-licenses", "--format=json", "--with-license-file"],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    packages = json.loads(result.stdout)

    report = {"tier1": [], "tier2": [], "tier3": [], "unknown": []}
    for pkg in packages:
        name = pkg["Name"]
        lic = pkg.get("License", "UNKNOWN")
        if any(t in lic for t in TIER1):
            report["tier1"].append({"name": name, "license": lic})
        elif any(t in lic for t in TIER2):
            report["tier2"].append({"name": name, "license": lic})
        elif any(t in lic for t in TIER3):
            report["tier3"].append({"name": name, "license": lic})
        else:
            report["unknown"].append({"name": name, "license": lic})

    return report


def check_oss_policy_gate(scan_report: dict) -> bool:
    """
    CI gate: fail the build if any Tier 3 dependencies are present
    without a recorded exception, or if unknown licenses exist.
    Returns True if the build should pass, False if it should fail.
    """
    # In practice, load approved exceptions from a file
    approved_exceptions = []  # e.g., ["some-agpl-package==1.2.3 (exception: isolated subprocess, no network exposure)"]

    tier3_without_exception = [
        p for p in scan_report["tier3"]
        if p["name"] not in approved_exceptions
    ]

    if tier3_without_exception:
        print("POLICY VIOLATION: Tier 3 (restricted) licenses found without approved exception:")
        for p in tier3_without_exception:
            print(f"  {p['name']}: {p['license']}")
        return False

    if scan_report["unknown"]:
        print("WARNING: Unknown licenses found — manual review required:")
        for p in scan_report["unknown"]:
            print(f"  {p['name']}: {p['license']}")
        # Unknown licenses block the build in strict mode; warn in permissive mode

    print(f"OSS policy gate passed: {len(scan_report['tier1'])} Tier1, "
          f"{len(scan_report['tier2'])} Tier2 (reviewed), "
          f"{len(scan_report['tier3'])} Tier3 exceptions approved.")
    return True

# Install license-checker for Node.js projects
npm install -g license-checker

# Generate a JSON report of all dependencies and their licenses
license-checker --json --out license-report.json

# Fail CI if any restricted licenses are found
license-checker --failOn "GPL;AGPL;SSPL" --excludePackages "approved-exception-package@1.2.3"

# For a Claude application, common packages to watch:
# - Some versions of elasticsearch-js ship with SSPL
# - Some annotation/labelling tools use AGPL
# - Always verify transitively: npm ls --json | check-licenses

All permissive licenses (Tier 1) require attribution — retaining the copyright notice and license text. For SaaS applications that do not distribute compiled binaries, attribution is typically fulfilled via an 'Open Source Acknowledgements' page or a
endpoint in the application. This page must be maintained as dependencies change.
Open-source acknowledgements page at a stable URL; often /about/open-source or /licenses
Page not updated when new dependencies are added; stale entries for removed dependencies
Embedded in the app's Settings > Acknowledgements screen; also in the App Store listing if required
Third-party notices file not included in the App bundle; automated generation not set up in build pipeline
NOTICE file in the binary distribution; --version output references the NOTICE
NOTICE file missing from release archives
NOTICE file in the installation package; referenced in the deployment guide
NOTICE file correct at release time but not updated on subsequent patches
Automating attribution file generation is the only practical approach at scale. Tools like
(general) generate attribution files that can be included in the build pipeline output automatically.

Claude applications frequently incorporate AI components beyond the Claude API itself: embedding models, vector databases, rerankers, OCR libraries, speech-to-text engines, and fine-tuned model weights. These components introduce OSS governance considerations beyond application code licensing:
Many open-source model weights are released under custom licenses that are not OSI-approved. Examples:
No special restrictions; follow standard Tier 1 obligations
Llama 3: commercial use permitted up to defined MAU threshold; attribution required; cannot use to train competing models
Usage restrictions: cannot generate certain content categories; RAIL (Responsible AI License) terms must be passed to downstream users
SaaS exposure risk if used in the data pipeline that runs as a service

RAIL (Responsible AI License) terms deserve particular attention: they include use restrictions that must be preserved in downstream deployments. If you build a product using a RAIL-licensed model, your users are also bound by the RAIL terms — and you are responsible for communicating and enforcing those restrictions.
Common OSS Compliance Scenarios in Claude Application Stacks
Claude applications share a predictable dependency stack. The following scenarios cover the most frequently encountered OSS compliance questions in this ecosystem:
Scenario 1: LangChain / LlamaIndex as Orchestration Framework
LangChain is MIT; LlamaIndex is MIT. Both are Tier 1 — approved without review.

However, both frameworks pull in numerous transitive dependencies. When you install LangChain, you indirectly depend on dozens of packages, some of which may carry non-permissive licenses depending on which optional integrations you enable. The OSS policy should require scanning transitive dependencies, not just direct ones.

# Check all transitive dependencies for your LangChain Claude integration
pip install pip-licenses
pip-licenses --format=table --order=license | grep -v "MIT\|Apache\|BSD\|ISC"

# For a requirements.txt approach, use pipdeptree to see the full tree
pip install pipdeptree
pipdeptree --warn silence | head -100

# Check for AGPL specifically (highest risk)
pip-licenses --format=json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
pkgs = json.load(sys.stdin)
risky = [p for p in pkgs if 'AGPL' in p.get('License', '') or 'GPL' in p.get('License', '')]
for p in risky:
    print(f"{p['Name']:40} {p['License']}")
"
Scenario 2: pgvector / Chroma / Weaviate as Vector Store
PostgreSQL License is OSI-approved permissive; no copyleft obligations
Weaviate Cloud is a separate commercial product — OSS governance applies to the client library, not the hosted service
No restrictions; check attu (admin UI) separately — also Apache 2.0
Elastic switched from Apache 2.0 to ELv2 + SSPL in 7.11. ELv2 restricts providing Elasticsearch as a managed service. SSPL path requires exception.
Created by AWS after the Elasticsearch license change; maintained as truly open-source
Scenario 3: Embedding Models in Claude Applications

Most Claude applications use embedding models for RAG. The embedding model is typically run via an API (OpenAI, Cohere) or self-hosted using a Hugging Face model. The license situation differs significantly:

When using an embedding model via an API, the model weights are not distributed — you are consuming a service. OSS license obligations for the model weights do not apply. The API client library (usually MIT or Apache 2.0) does.

Model licenses on Hugging Face vary widely. SBERT models are Apache 2.0. E5 models are MIT. Some models carry custom research-only licenses or RAIL terms. Always check the model card's 'License' field before self-hosting.
Fine-tuned models based on restricted base models.

If you fine-tune a model whose base weights carry a Llama Community License, your fine-tuned derivative is also subject to that license. You cannot release the fine-tuned weights under a more permissive license.

Manual license review does not scale. An OSS usage policy only provides reliable protection when it is enforced automatically in the CI/CD pipeline, blocking merges that introduce unapproved licenses.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_oss_exception_request(package_info: dict, business_justification: str) -> str:
    """
    Generate a structured OSS exception request for Legal review,
    for a Tier 3 dependency that the engineering team needs to use.
    """
    prompt = f"""You are an open-source compliance specialist. Write a structured OSS license
exception request for Legal review based on the package information and business
justification below.

Package:
{json.dumps(package_info, indent=2)}

Business justification from engineering team:
{business_justification}

Write a structured exception request document containing:
1. Executive summary (2 sentences): what is being requested and why
2. License risk assessment: what specific obligations does this license create?
   What is the exposure if we use it without an exception?
3. Proposed isolation measures: technical steps that reduce the copyleft risk
   (e.g., process isolation, replacing the component at the API boundary,
   ensuring the component is not distributed)
4. Alternative analysis: did you evaluate permissively-licensed alternatives?
   Why are they insufficient?
5. Recommended approval conditions: specific conditions Legal should impose
   if granting the exception (e.g., "must run as isolated subprocess",
   "must not be bundled in distributed client")
6. Risk if exception is denied: what is the impact on the project if Legal says no?

Tone: professional, technically precise, assumes Legal has general counsel expertise
but not deep software license expertise."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text


pkg = {
    "name": "faiss-cpu",
    "version": "1.7.4",
    "license": "MIT",  # faiss is actually MIT — but let's use a hypothetical
    "actual_license_being_requested": "GPL 3.0",
    "homepage": "https://github.com/facebookresearch/faiss",
    "used_for": "Vector similarity search in our RAG pipeline",
    "dependency_type": "direct",
    "how_used": "Called as a library from our Python backend; not distributed to end users"
}

justification = """
We need FAISS for approximate nearest-neighbour search in our RAG pipeline.
The only alternatives at this scale are commercial (Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud)
which would cost $15k/month at our current query volume. The open-source
alternatives (annoy, hnswlib) have significantly worse recall at our required
latency budget. FAISS is run server-side only and never distributed to customers.
"""

print(generate_oss_exception_request(pkg, justification))

# .github/workflows/oss-license-check.yml
name: OSS License Compliance

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  license-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt pip-licenses

      - name: Generate license report
        run: pip-licenses --format=json --output-file=license-report.json

      - name: Upload license report as artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: license-report
          path: license-report.json

      - name: Check for restricted licenses
        run: |
          python3 -c "
          import json, sys

          RESTRICTED = ['GNU General Public License', 'GNU Affero', 'Server Side Public License', 'SSPL']
          EXCEPTIONS_FILE = '.oss-exceptions.json'

          with open('license-report.json') as f:
              packages = json.load(f)

          try:
              with open(EXCEPTIONS_FILE) as f:
                  exceptions = json.load(f)
          except FileNotFoundError:
              exceptions = {}

          violations = []
          for pkg in packages:
              lic = pkg.get('License', '')
              name = pkg['Name']
              if any(r in lic for r in RESTRICTED):
                  if name not in exceptions:
                      violations.append(f'{name}: {lic}')

          if violations:
              print('OSS POLICY VIOLATION — restricted licenses without approved exception:')
              for v in violations:
                  print(f'  {v}')
              sys.exit(1)

          print('OSS license check passed.')
          "
When your engineers modify an open-source dependency (patching a bug, adding a feature), additional obligations arise depending on the license tier:
No obligation to contribute changes back
Best practice: contribute bug fixes upstream to reduce maintenance burden; not legally required
Modifications to the LGPL/MPL-licensed files themselves must be made available; your application code is not affected
Maintain a fork of the modified component and publish it; link to the fork in your NOTICE file
Modifications must be released under the same license; for AGPL, network service users are entitled to the source
If an exception has been granted, confirm the exception's conditions on modification disclosure; typically the exception covers unmodified use only

A common inner-source mistake is to patch a dependency inline (in a vendor/ folder or node_modules/) without tracking the modification. When the dependency is updated, the patch is silently lost. Use proper forking and patch management (e.g., patch-package for Node.js, git subtree for Python) to track modifications and ensure compliance.
Handling OSS Governance for AI-Assisted Code Generation

Claude can generate code that includes or resembles open-source code. This creates a distinct governance question: if Claude generates code that is substantially similar to GPL-licensed code (because that code was in Claude's training data), does the generated output carry GPL obligations?

Anthropic's current position, based on their usage policies, is that Claude's outputs are the user's work product and do not inherit the licenses of training data. However, this area remains legally unsettled. Practical enterprise governance steps:
Code review processes should flag generated code that appears to be verbatim or near-verbatim copies of known OSS libraries — this is a risk signal regardless of the legal theory.
Use of OSS scanning tools on generated code.
Tools like BlackDuck, FOSSA, or Snyk can detect code patterns that match known open-source projects and flag potential copyright concerns.
For code included in distributed products, document whether it was AI-generated — this supports the claim that the code is original output rather than a reproduction of a specific licensed work.

An open-source usage policy for Claude applications classifies licenses into three tiers: permissive licenses (MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD) approved without review; weak-copyleft licenses (LGPL, MPL) approved with legal review; and strong-copyleft licenses (GPL, AGPL, SSPL) requiring explicit exception. AGPL is the highest-risk license for SaaS Claude applications — a single AGPL dependency can trigger an obligation to release all application source. Attribution obligations must be fulfilled for all Tier 1 licenses and automated via build tooling. Third-party AI components introduce additional governance complexity through custom model licenses (Llama Community License, RAIL) that impose use restrictions beyond standard OSS terms. Automated scanning integrated into CI pipelines prevents unlicensed dependencies from reaching production without review.
